reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer demand for school-leaver competence (numeracy, literacy, problem-solving, teamwork) regardless of intake sorting; labour-market interest in efficient talent matching and reduced credential inflation; tech-sector need for diverse cognitive diversity and non-elite pipeline talent.
reactions.interest: Employers and the tech sector — need a wider, more diverse talent supply pipeline; current early-selection mechanism wastes low-SES high-ability pupils and narrows the applicant pool.
reactions.public_good_frame: Efficient human-capital development: school selection should identify and develop talent, not reproduce family background. A less SES-skewed intake into selective tracks preserves merit-based selection (a public good) while widening the pool of candidates employers can recruit from.
reactions.argument: S1 preserves selective tracks but decouples entry from paid test-prep, which serves employer interest: a less-coachable assessment is more likely to surface ability independent of family resources. Regional equity quotas mechanically widen the applicant pool into selective tracks — this is good for employers because it expands the high-achieving cohort we can recruit from later. However, the proposal carries unresolved implementation risks: (1) SES-proxy definition is flagged as unresolved and gameable — if the indicator fails, the quota effect collapses and we return to SES-skewed selection; (2) non-state maintainer compliance is negotiated, not legislated, so selective demand may simply migrate to unregulated schools; (3) a less-coachable assessment requires genuine psychometric development (not a low cost) and may not actually resist informal selection (interviews, social screening) that reproduces sorting differently. If these risks materialize, we are paying high transition costs for no actual pipeline gain. Conditional support: the stance becomes support only if (a) the SES-proxy is piloted and shown to be non-gameable at application time, (b) non-state maintainers are bound uniformly via subsidy conditionality, and (c) the new assessment is validated to resist informal selection mechanisms in real schools.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support if: (1) SES-proxy piloting demonstrates non-gameable definition and cross-validation with registry data; (2) church and foundation maintainers agree in writing to uniform equity-quota admission rules conditional on subsidy; (3) the new assessment is psychometrically validated against informal-selection risks (interview, reputation effects). Oppose if: (1) SES-proxy fails piloting or is flagged as gameable; (2) non-state maintainers opt out and selective demand migrates there; (3) the reformed assessment still correlates strongly with test-prep intensity or parental education.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer labour-market interest in talent identification and retention; tech-sector interest in expanding the high-ability pool; evidence-based policy preference for mechanisms with measured outcomes.
reactions.interest: Employers and the tech sector — we need a stable, predictable pipeline of high-ability school-leavers. Shrinking selective track entry without changing admission rules risks shrinking the absolute number of high-achievers we can recruit from, while concentrating remaining seats among higher-SES applicants (residual-skew effect).
reactions.public_good_frame: Efficient sorting is a public good: the labour market benefits when schools identify and develop talent. Arbitrary caps that reduce selection intensity without improving selection accuracy are economically wasteful — they shrink the pool we recruit from without addressing SES-skew.
reactions.argument: S2's mechanical cap effect is clear: fewer entry places means fewer early-sorted pupils. But this is economically backwards from an employer perspective. The scenario does not propose to improve selection accuracy — it simply removes seats. Because the cap is not paired with admission reform (S1), the residual-skew effect is near-certain: as seats shrink, the same SES-skewed demand compresses into a smaller pool, so surviving applicants are even more skewed toward high-SES families (who have information and preparation resources). This harms employer interest in two ways: (1) we lose absolute talent — some high-ability low-SES pupils who would have entered the selective track under the old rules never enter; (2) we gain no improvement in sorting accuracy, so the claim that 'peer composition improves in general schools' is speculative and rests on the contested asymmetric-peer-effect assumption, which this proposal does not validate. The advanced-programme funding in general schools is a weak proxy for the claimed peer-benefit and does not target ability directly. Finally, the scenario flags a reversibility risk (Polish precedent): if a successor government unfreezes caps, we have paid transition costs for temporary volatility in our recruitment pool. From an employer standpoint, S2 is a policy that shrinks the talent supply without improving selection quality, contradicts our interest in efficient talent matching, and creates political instability around the pipeline.
reactions.condition_to_change: Oppose unless: (1) the proposal is paired with S1-type admission reform so that caps improve selection accuracy as well as shrink slots; (2) evidence demonstrates that the asymmetric peer-effect actually materializes in pilot general schools (i.e. measured peer-composition gain in high-achieving non-selective cohorts); (3) an entrenchment mechanism (supermajority, multi-year notice) is legislated to stabilize the cap trajectory across government changes. Without all three, S2 trades talent supply for political symbolism.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in long-term human-capital development and addressing teacher shortage at secondary level; tech-sector interest in diverse talent development and late-blooming patterns; evidence-based policy preference when cross-country evidence is available.
reactions.interest: Employers and the tech sector — we need a wider, more diverse high-ability cohort at age 14-18, and we need robust secondary-level teaching in STEM and literacy to sustain it. Early selection at ages 10-12 is inefficient sorting: many pupils are misjudged and many high-ability low-SES pupils never apply. Later selection at 14, paired with stronger teaching, could improve both accuracy and equity.
reactions.public_good_frame: Delayed selection improves the quality of sorting: age-14 choices are better-informed and less SES-skewed than age-10. Equity and efficiency align here — a broader pool of high-achievers is a larger talent supply for employers and a fairer outcome for society.
reactions.argument: S3 rests on moderate cross-country evidence (Finland, Poland) that delayed selection improves equity without harming high-achievers' outcomes, contingent on bundling with teacher investment and diagnostic support. From an employer standpoint, this is attractive: later, better-informed sorting at 14 should reduce misallocation of talent and expand the absolute pool of identifiable high-achievers. The mechanism is plausible: age 10 is too early to assess ability independently of family background; age 14 is more reliable. However, the proposal carries a critical load-bearing risk: sorting migration. If middle-class and high-SES families respond by exiting to non-state or private schools, the aggregate equity gain could be illusory — the SES-outcome gradient might narrow only because high-SES pupils leave the public system, concentrating disadvantage in general schools and harming the low-SES pupils left behind. The scenario flags this as the largest risk but treats it as an assumption ('sorting does not migrate') rather than a tested mechanism. From an employer perspective, this migration risk is critical because it determines whether the broader talent pool is actually accessible to us: if sorting relocates, we do not gain a diverse pipeline; we lose visibility into low-SES talent and face a bifurcated labour-market where public-system graduates are systematically lower-SES. Second, the proposal depends on teacher supply — heterogeneous de-tracked classrooms require more sophisticated teaching than tracked systems. The scenario flags teacher shortage as a binding constraint; if de-tracked classes degrade in quality due to teacher shortage, the benefit is forfeited and the reform risks delegitimizing comprehensive systems, inviting reversal. Conditional support: the stance becomes support only if a pilot in 2-3 districts measures sorting-migration explicitly and finds that non-state exit does not hollow out the general-school peer pool. Without that evidence, S3 risks concentrating disadvantage without improving talent identification.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support if: (1) pilot evaluation in 2-3 diverse districts (urban, rural, mixed-SES) measures whether non-state/private exit actually occurs and whether general-school peer composition improves or worsens; (2) teacher supply audit confirms sufficient workforce for heterogeneous de-tracked classes in pilot areas, with retraining completion tracked; (3) a 5-year pilot explicitly measures the SES-outcome gradient in pilot versus comparison cohorts, with subgroup analysis for pupils who remain in the public system. Oppose if: (1) sorting visibly migrates to non-state maintainers during pilot, concentrating disadvantage in general schools; (2) teacher shortage forces class sizes or ability grouping that re-creates de facto tracking; (3) cross-party pact fails and no entrenchment mechanism is legislated (reversal risk makes the sunk costs unacceptable).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Employer interest in talent identification efficiency; evidence-based policy preference for mechanisms with measured outcomes and sustainable budgets; labour-market interest in reducing credential inflation and signalling noise.
reactions.interest: Employers and the tech sector — we need a stable, growing pool of well-developed talent. Compensation schemes that leave the sorting mechanism intact risk legitimising inefficient selection and creating false credentialing (good grades in disadvantaged schools signal differently than grades in selective tracks).
reactions.public_good_frame: Efficient human-capital development requires both accurate sorting and equitable access. Compensation that masks rather than offsets sorting creates credential noise and signalling failure in the labour market — employers cannot rely on grades or school prestige as a reliable ability signal when the system bifurcates.
reactions.argument: S4 is the 'keep structure, compensate' option: leave the gimnázium tracks intact, raise standards in general schools, and fund targeted support for disadvantaged schools. The proposal rests on the Portuguese case, where non-structural TEIP-style packages improved outcomes. However, Portugal did not have early selection at ages 10-12; it had selection by prior achievement at secondary entry (age 10-11 in some regions, but with lower stakes and less SES-skew). The load-bearing assumption — that compensation can offset (rather than mask) the effects of Hungary's early, SES-skewed sorting — is flagged in the scenario as unevidenced and unsupported by any comparable country case. From an employer standpoint, this is critical: if compensation only masks the effect of sorting (pupil stays in general track due to low SES, receives support, achieves grade X), but the same pupil in a selective track would have achieved grade Y, then the compensation has not closed the gap — it has only made the gap less visible. This creates a credential-signalling problem for employers: a grade A from a disadvantaged general school is not commensurable with a grade A from a selective track, because the pools were sorted, not improved. The scenario's own political-risk section flags a second critical weakness: 'compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation.' This means the package is not politically sustainable — it depends on the government of the day choosing to protect the budget when fiscal pressure mounts. No employer can plan a recruitment strategy around a budget line that is politically vulnerable and the first to be cut. S4 thus fails on two grounds: (1) it does not address the sorting mechanism, so it either masks inequality (creating credential noise) or legitimises early selection permanently (entrenching the gradient); (2) it depends on a budget commitment that the scenario itself concedes is the most vulnerable in fiscal cycles. Neither outcome serves employer interest in a stable, identifiable talent pipeline.
reactions.condition_to_change: Oppose unless: (1) a pilot demonstrates that pupils in disadvantaged general schools receiving the TEIP-style package achieve outcomes (in maths, literacy, tertiary entry, labour-market earnings) comparable to pupils in selective tracks — i.e. compensation actually offsets sorting, not just masks it; (2) the compensation budget is legislatively ring-fenced (multi-year appropriation, supermajority reversal requirement) to protect it from fiscal-cycle cuts; (3) a second pilot in a district with teacher shortage conditions shows that the standards-and-support package can be delivered without depressing academic rigour (i.e. the teacher-shortage risk is shown to be manageable). Without all three, S4 is a budget-vulnerable masking operation, not a sustainable human-capital strategy.
